original_abstract:
In 1972, Erich Clar introduced his aromatic π-sextet rule, providing a qualitative yet powerful framework for understanding the aromatic character of benzenoid systems. Over four decades later, this deceptively simple model remains a vital source of inspiration and a cornerstone for researchers in the field. Its utility has been extensively validated through both experimental observations—such as X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy, and advanced microscopy (STM/AFM)—and rigorous theoretical computations. 

In this review, we examine the enduring success of Clar’s rule by highlighting its application across diverse chemical environments. We specifically focus on its ability to predict the local aromaticity and reactivity of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and we discuss two significant recent applications: the analysis of resonance-assisted hydrogen bonding and the justification of the emerging biradical character in higher acenes. Despite some inherent limitations, Clar’s rule continues to be an essential tool for navigating the complexities of sp2-bonded carbon materials.
humanized_abstract:
It was in 1972 that the aromatic π-sextet rule was introduced by Erich Clar. Forty years on this fairly qualitative model is still a major source of inspiration for the understanding of the aromaticity of benzenoid species. The validity of this model has been demonstrated by a large amount of experimental evidence: X ray analyses, NMR, STM and AFM microscopies, as well as by theoretical investigations, shown in this volume.

This perspective aims at explaining the longevity of Clar’s rule and shows its applicability in a variety of chemical situations. Its application to predict the local aromaticity and reactivity in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and also to understand two of the most recent developments in PAH chemistry, namely, resonance-assisted hydrogen bonding and the emerging biradical character in higher acenes are illustrated. Even with its known and sometimes unknown limitations, Clar’s rule remains a fundamental and useful tool for dealing with the sp2 hybridized carbon materials.

Even with its known and sometimes unknown limitations, Clar’s rule remains a fundamental and useful tool for dealing with the sp2 hybridized carbon materials.
